Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a routine. I'd study when I felt like it or when something was due soon. Sometimes I'd go a whole week barely touching my coursework, then cram a bunch of hours in when I realized I was falling behind. My 28 hours a week sounds like a lot, but it was really scattered and unfocused most of the time.
Q2: It varied a lot depending on what else was going on. If I had extracurricular stuff or just wasn't feeling it, studying would get pushed aside. Deadlines were really the only thing that got me to sit down consistently. I never had like a set Tuesday afternoon study session or anything structured like that.
Q3: Usually it was because I needed to check when something was due or download materials for an assignment. Sometimes I'd get a notification that something was posted and I'd log in just to see what it was. I wasn't the type to browse around the LMS just to explore or stay on top of things proactively.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. It never became automatic for me. I'd remember to check it when I had to, but it always felt like a chore rather than something natural. Maybe if the interface was more engaging or if I actually cared more about the content, it would've been different.
Q5: The videos were probably the most useful because I learn better by watching and doing rather than reading. I watched a decent amount of video content when I actually sat down to study. Discussion posts were okay too because sometimes other students would explain things in ways that clicked better for me than the lectures.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the readings and supplementary materials. They felt like extra work that I didn't have time or energy for. If it wasn't directly going to be on an exam or assignment, I figured why bother. I also didn't really engage with practice quizzes much because they felt optional.
Q7: If I could see a direct connection to passing the course or getting a better grade, then it felt worth it. Anything that seemed like busywork or wasn't clearly tied to assessment, I'd skip. I needed to see immediate relevance, otherwise my motivation just wasn't there to put in the effort.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff basically didn't exist for me. If it was required, I'd do it eventually, even if I procrastinated. But optional activities? I'd tell myself I'd get to them later and then never did. There's just not enough motivation when nothing's on the line.
Q9: Definitely. I'd download lecture slides and videos and study from those on my phone or laptop without being connected to the LMS. Sometimes I'd work through problems with a friend in person, or I'd use YouTube videos to understand concepts better. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the platform.
Q10: There were times I'd spend hours going over downloaded notes or working through practice problems on paper. None of that showed up anywhere online. Or I'd have study sessions with classmates where we'd talk through material together. The LMS only sees clicks, not actual understanding.
Q11: Yeah, all the time. I'd download a video or PDF once and then use it multiple times offline. My internet at home isn't great and I have limited data sometimes, so it made more sense to grab stuff once and work with it locally. The LMS would just show one access but I'd use that material for days.
Q12: Around midterms things got really hard to keep up with. I had extracurricular commitments piling up and I just felt disconnected from the course. It wasn't that the material was too hard, I just couldn't bring myself to care enough. Everything felt like it was happening to someone else.
Q13: For sure. When I was tired or had other responsibilities, the LMS was the first thing to get neglected. If I had a busy week with my extracurriculars, I'd barely log in at all. Confusion also played a role—when I didn't understand something, I'd avoid it rather than ask for help.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd open videos or readings and just zone out completely. I'd be technically watching something but thinking about other stuff entirely. My mind would wander and I'd realize I absorbed nothing. Those sessions were basically wasted time even though they'd show as activity.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they were important. I think it's because the immediate pressure wasn't there until the deadline got close. Also, sometimes I just didn't feel capable of doing a good job, so avoiding it felt easier than trying and failing.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. I'd see a deadline coming up and suddenly I'd be on the LMS downloading everything, watching videos at 2x speed, trying to piece things together. Without deadlines, I probably wouldn't have engaged at all. They created artificial urgency I needed.
Q17: Feedback didn't really change much for me. By the time I got it, I'd already mentally moved on to the next thing. If the feedback was positive, cool. If it was negative, I'd feel bad for a minute but not really change my approach. I know that's not great but that's how it was.
Q18: The course structure was pretty standard, which meant it was easy to fall into patterns of neglect. There weren't many checkpoints forcing me to stay engaged throughout. If there had been more frequent small deadlines or interactive elements, maybe I would've stayed more connected to the material.
Q19: The lack of hands-on activities made it harder for me since I learn better by doing. Everything felt very passive—read this, watch that. If there had been more practical exercises or simulations, I think I would've been more engaged. The discussion forums helped a bit but weren't enough.
Q20: I think some students might be searching for information or trying to understand what's expected without actually completing the work. Maybe they're overwhelmed and don't know where to start, so they keep clicking around hoping something will click. Or they're just procrastinating productively, if that makes sense.
Q21: That's kind of me, actually. I didn't log in much but I managed a Merit grade because I studied effectively offline and did okay on exams. The LMS activity doesn't capture studying from downloaded materials or learning from peers. You can engage with content without constantly being logged in.
Q22: Logs can show when someone accessed stuff and how often, but they can't show understanding or effort. They miss offline studying, group work, and mental engagement. Someone could click through everything without learning anything, or learn a lot without clicking much. It's a pretty incomplete picture.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in genuine effort to understand it. It's not just showing up or clicking through materials. For me, I wasn't really engaged most of the time—I was just doing enough to get by. Real engagement would mean being curious and invested.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show that I was dealing with low motivation throughout the course, or that I was juggling extracurriculars with limited resources. It wouldn't capture the CBT sessions I had or how my emotional state affected my studying. It also misses all the offline learning and the times I was physically present but mentally checked out.
